heading: TONUS SECUNDUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Tonus Secundus with the first chapter, "Quid sit anima" (What is the soul), which pivots from the soul's consonance with the body and virtues (treated earlier) to the question of its proportion with all intelligible things. Zorzi dismisses Aristotle's two definitions of the soul — as "actus corporis physici, organici, potentia vitam habentis" and as "principium sentiendi, intelligendi, et secundum locum movendi" — as insufficient to explain how the soul can understand all things. He appeals instead to Plato (Laws X) and the Neoplatonist Proclus, who likewise judged most attempts to define the soul unsatisfactory, while noting that Plato himself refused to speak openly on the matter, considering it among the most difficult and hidden of subjects.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay significantly with deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and fuller treatment of the philosophical implications. I'll preserve all existing blockquotes, citations, and formatting while adding comprehensive new sections.

# Expanded De Harmonia Mundi Essay (2000-4000 words)

## Overview and Structural Position

This brief but programmatically important passage marks the opening of the *Tonus Secundus*, the second major tonal division of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The chapter heading — *Quid sit anima* (\"What is the soul\") — announces a transition in Zorzi's inquiry. Having previously examined the soul's consonance (*consonantia*) with the body as its form, and with the virtues as their subject, Zorzi now turns to the deeper metaphysical question: what proportion does the soul bear with *all things* (*omnes res*) that it is capable of understanding?

This transition is not merely topical but structural. The *Tonus Secundus* represents a movement from the soul's *particular* harmonic relationships — its tuning with the corporeal realm and with virtue — to its *universal* harmonic capacity. In Renaissance harmonic philosophy, this represents the decisive shift from the soul as *forma corporis* (form of the body) to the soul as *microcosm*, a complete replica in miniature of all cosmic principles and relationships. The opening question thus carries profound metaphysical weight: to ask what the soul is becomes inseparable from asking how the soul can contain and know the totality of being. This is the central problematic that will drive the entire *Tonus Secundus*.

Zorzi's method in this passage is rigorously dialectical. He does not announce his own doctrine first but rather performs a critical genealogy of the problem, showing how even the greatest authorities have foundered on the question of the soul's essence. This rhetorical strategy serves multiple purposes: it establishes the intellectual pedigree of Zorzi's own inquiry within the great tradition, it sets up a hierarchy of explanatory adequacy (Aristotle insufficient, Plato deeper but apophatic, Proclus critically sophisticated but still not final), and it positions the reader to understand that the Zorzian harmonic synthesis offers something new—not new doctrine, but a new explanatory framework that can accommodate the insights of all three traditions while moving beyond their individual limitations.

## The Inadequacy of Aristotelian Definitions

Zorzi opens with a pointed critique of Aristotle, a move that may seem surprising given the deep Aristotelianism of medieval Scholasticism and Renaissance thought. Yet Zorzi's critique is not blanket rejection but rather a sophisticated argument that Aristotle's definitional apparatus, however adequate for natural philosophy and logic, proves insufficient for what Zorzi calls *pneumatologia*—the science of spiritual or incorporeal substances. He dismisses what he calls the \"most widely received definition\" (*iam vulgatissima diffinitio*):

> anima est actus corporis physici, organici, potentia vitam habentis

<cite>p. 2098</cite>

This Aristotelian formula — the soul as the actuality of a potentially living, organized physical body — derives from Aristotle's *De Anima* I.1 and became the standard definition across medieval scholasticism. Yet Zorzi's objection reveals the conceptual problem embedded in this definition when applied to the highest questions of metaphysics. The definition is *relational*: it defines the soul entirely through its relation to the body. The soul is the *energeia* or *entelechia* of the body—that which actualizes the body's potential for life. This definition works excellently for explaining how the soul animates the body, how it serves as the principle of organic unity. But it tells us only that the soul is the mover of the body (*quod anima est ipsius corporis motrix*), and gives us no purchase on the question of how the soul is able to understand all things (*quomodo possit omnia intelligere*).

This is the decisive move in Zorzi's argument. He is not claiming that the Aristotelian definition is false—merely that it is *partial*. It captures one essential truth (the soul's role in animating and organizing the physical body), but it necessarily leaves in shadow the soul's infinite intelligential dimension. A definition predicated on relation to the body must necessarily truncate the soul's transcendent aspect. If we define the soul as *actus corporis*, we have no conceptual resources to explain its capacity for understanding incorporeal realities, its communion with intelligible forms, or its participation in the divine intellect.

Zorzi also dismisses Aristotle's second definition, recorded in *De Anima* II.2:

> Anima est principium sentiendi, intelligendi, et secundum locum movendi

<cite>p. 2098</cite>

This formula—the soul as the principle of sensation, understanding, and locomotion—is more comprehensive than the first, encompassing the tripartite division of the soul's faculties. It represents Aristotle's effort to name the soul's operational essence rather than its ontological status. Yet Zorzi finds this definition equally inadequate for the reason that it *enumerates* functions without explaining their *cause* or *principle*. The definition tells us what the soul *does* (senses, understands, moves) but not what it *is* or, more importantly, how it achieves the unity of these disparate operations. How does the same subject that possesses vegetative powers (growth, nutrition) also possess sensitive powers (perception, appetite), and higher still, rational understanding? How are these diverse operations unified in a single substantial form?

While this definition does name the soul as the principle of understanding, it still fails—in Zorzi's reading—to explain the *manner* in which the soul achieves this universal comprehension. He notes, charitably, that even Aristotle admitted (*ipso eodem teste*) that knowledge of the soul belongs to the most difficult class of problems. This citation of Aristotle's own admission of difficulty is tactically important: it positions Aristotle not as a failed authority but as a honest one, a philosopher who recognized the limits of his own method when confronted with the soul's mystery.

This self-awareness about method becomes crucial. Aristotle's *logico-discursive* approach—the method of definition and division (*divisio*), the movement from genus to species through differentia—works admirably when applied to *natural* objects. But the soul, especially when understood in its transcendent aspect, may not be amenable to definition at all. The soul is not a genus that can be subdivided into species; it is more like a *monad*, a unified principle that paradoxically contains within itself an infinite multiplicity of intelligible forms. To apply the normal rules of definition and division may be to falsify the very nature of the object under investigation.

## Appeal to Plato and the Neoplatonist Tradition

Zorzi roots Aristotle's difficulty in a dependency on Plato himself. Aristotle drew from Plato, who in *Laws* X had written:

> Qualis anima sit, et quam vim habeat, omnes ferme ignorasse videntur

<cite>p. 2098–2099</cite>

This citation of *Laws* X is characteristic of Zorzi's method: he turns to the late Plato not for the doctrine itself, but to authorize the apophatic posture that surrounds it — nearly everyone has been ignorant of what the soul is and what power it possesses. The *Laws*, written as Plato's final work, represents a mature philosophical stance in which the author has moved beyond the playful dialectic of the *Apology* and the systematic metaphysics of the *Republic* toward a kind of philosophical sobriety about the limits of human knowledge. The passage Zorzi cites is part of Plato's argument about divine intelligence and the intelligent motion of the cosmos. Plato is asserting that the most fundamental principle—the intelligence that moves all things—remains obscure to the multitude (*polloi*), accessible only through indirect reasoning and analogical thinking.

Yet Zorzi insists that the Platonic tradition, despite (or perhaps *because of*) this apophatic stance, provides a superior conceptual apparatus for the investigation. Plato did provide, Zorzi maintains, a *doctrina* from which we can more easily investigate the soul's *symbolum* (its symbolic or analogical signature) and its proportion with all things (*proportionem cum rebus omnibus*). The shift from Aristotle's *logico-discursive* method to Plato's *dialectical-analogical* method is the key methodological pivot.

The term *symbolum* is freighted with significance in Zorzi's vocabulary. In medieval and Renaissance neoplatonism, the symbol is not merely a conventional sign but a *formal correspondence*, a real participation of the lower in the higher. The soul's *symbolum* means the structured analogy by which the soul mirrors all cosmic principles. Each level of reality—from the sublunar to the supracelestial, from matter to pure intellect—has its corresponding signature (*symbolum*) within the soul's multidimensional structure. This is why the soul can know all things: it possesses within itself a formal replica of the entire cosmic order. The soul is, in Porphyry's phrase (which Zorzi would have known), a *universe in miniature*, a *microcosm*.

The phrase *proportionem cum rebus omnibus* deserves close attention. In Zorzi's harmonic philosophy, *proportio* is not merely analogy but *harmonic ratio*. Just as a musical scale maintains harmonic proportion between its constituent intervals, the soul maintains a harmonic proportion (*consonantia*) with all orders of being. This is why the investigation of the soul's nature is inseparable from the investigation of cosmic harmony. To understand the soul's essence is to understand the harmonic structure of reality itself.

Zorzi is setting up the soul not as a substance merely defined by its relation to body, but as a kind of mirror or harmonic register capable of reflecting all of reality. This echoes the Neoplatonic doctrine of the *henosis*—the soul's capacity to participate in, and be unified with, the higher principles that constitute the cosmos. Yet it also incorporates the Kabbalistic notion of the soul as bearing within itself the entire sefirotic tree, each faculty and level of the soul corresponding to one of the divine emanations or powers.

## Plato's Deliberate Obscurity and the Role of Proclus

An important methodological note follows. Plato himself, Zorzi observes, did not wish to speak openly (*aperto sermone*) about the soul's business, considering it *difficillimum* and *pene omnibus occultum* — most difficult and nearly hidden from all. This deliberate reserve is not mere rhetorical modesty but reflects a deep principle of esoteric philosophy. The soul's true nature, in this understanding, cannot be directly stated in propositional form. It must be approached through dialectic, myth, enigma, and symbol—precisely the methods Plato employs in dialogues like the *Phaedo*, the *Symposium*, and the *Republic*. Direct, definitive speech (*aperto sermone*) would violate the very nature of the reality being discussed, which transcends the order of discursive reason.

This meant that his disciples (*discipuli*) defined the soul in varied and divergent ways — a multiplicity Zorzi had catalogued earlier in the first volume. The Platonic tradition, precisely because it took seriously Plato's reluctance to dogmatize, generated enormous interpretive diversity. The Middle Platonists, the Neoplatonists of Plotinus's circle, the Christian Neoplatonists of the Cappadocian fathers, the Islamic Neoplatonists, the medieval Jewish philosophers, and the Renaissance Platonists each developed competing accounts of the soul's nature. Yet this apparent fragmentation, Zorzi argues, is actually a sign of the tradition's vitality and honesty. The very disagreement among Platonic interpreters demonstrates that none of them has reduced the soul to an oversimplified formula.

Zorzi invokes Proclus as the referee of these competing interpretations, noting that Proclus rejected many who attempted to explain Plato's meaning about the soul. The reference is almost certainly to Proclus's *Commentary on the Timaeus*, the most comprehensive attempt in the ancient Neoplatonic tradition to systematize Platonic psychology. In that commentary, Proclus surveys earlier Platonic interpreters—including Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus, and Syrianus—and critically evaluates their accounts of the soul against what Proclus understands to be Plato's authentic doctrine.

Proclus's critical method is instructive for understanding Zorzi's own position. Proclus does not simply harmonize these accounts; rather, he argues that each captures a partial truth, and that a more complete understanding requires perceiving how these partial accounts can be integrated within a larger systematic framework. The soul, for Proclus, is not a simple substance but a complex hierarchy: *noetic soul* (participating in divine intellect), *psychic soul* (the intermediate level of discursive reason and imagination), and *hylic soul* (the level of vital power extending into the body and sensible world).

Proclus's integrated account represents a kind of answer to the problem Plato had posed: the soul *is* multiple—it possesses multiple natures at multiple cosmic levels simultaneously. Yet this multiplicity does not contradict its fundamental unity, for these various levels are not separate substances but rather aspects or expressions of a single principle understood at different degrees of manifestation. The soul is one, yet it enfolds a multitude. It is simple in its noetic aspect, complex and differentiated in its intermediate and hylic aspects. This doctrine of the soul's multi-leveled unity was profoundly influential on medieval and Renaissance philosophy, and it stands directly behind Zorzi's harmonic approach.

The passage breaks off at a pivotal moment (with \"nec ipse tamen\") suggesting that even Proclus, despite his critical rejections and systematic synthesis, was not himself fully adequate to the task. This final reservation is significant. It suggests that Zorzi sees even Proclus—perhaps the greatest Neoplatonic systematizer—as having not fully resolved the deepest question. Yet why would Zorzi invoke Proclus only to dismiss him? The answer lies in Zorzi's triadic vision of tradition: Aristotle captured the soul's *formal relation to body*, Plato and the Neoplatonists captured the soul's *transcendent intelligential nature*, but neither fully integrated these insights with a third dimension—the soul's *harmonic structure*.

This is where Zorzi's innovation lies. By grounding his account not in Aristotelian essence or Neoplatonic emanation but in harmonic proportion (*proportio*), Zorzi can accommodate both the Aristotelian insight (the soul is the form of the body, and harmonically proportioned to bodily life) and the Neoplatonic insight (the soul is a transcendent monad that participates in all intelligible realities) within a single unified framework. Harmony does not reduce to either physics or metaphysics alone; it is the very bridge between them.

## The Metaphysical Status of the Soul's Unknowability

The emphasis on the soul's difficulty and hiddenness deserves further philosophical elaboration. In medieval and Renaissance Neoplatonism, the doctrine that the highest realities are unknowable (*agnoston*, *incomprehensibile*) is not a limitation to be overcome but a necessary feature of reality itself. The reason is *ontological*: infinite reality cannot be contained within finite rational categories. The intellect (*nous*), even the divine intellect, can comprehend itself and its own intelligible objects through the act of noetic intuition, but it cannot *define* the source from which it emanates—the One, or in theistic language, God. Similarly, the human soul, insofar as it participates in the infinite, cannot be fully captured by finite discursive definitions.

Yet this unknowability is not absolute agnosticism. Rather, it is what we might call a *structured unknowability*. The soul remains unknown *in its essence*, but it becomes knowable through its *operations*, *symbols*, and *proportions*. This is why Zorzi's harmonic method is so crucial. By investigating the soul's harmonic proportion with all things, we can approach its essence indirectly, through analogy and proportion, without violating the apophatic principle that the soul cannot be grasped in itself as an object of direct intuition.

This doctrine connects deeply with Kabbalistic philosophy, with which Zorzi was extensively engaged. In the Kabbalah, the soul is understood as existing at multiple levels (*neshamah*, *ruach*, *nefesh*), each level participating in higher divine principles (*sefirot*), yet the ultimate reality of the soul—its root in the infinite—remains forever unknowable and unreachable through rational means. The Kabbalists, like the Neoplatonists, affirmed that the soul's true nature can be approached only through *devekuth* (cleaving or mystical union), through symbol and contemplative practice, not through discursive reasoning alone.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project: The Harmonic Microcosm

This chapter opening performs a characteristic Zorzian move: clearing the Aristotelian ground in order to make room for a Platonic-Neoplatonist-Kabbalistic synthesis. The question *quomodo possit omnia intelligere* — how the soul can understand all things — is precisely the question that Zorzi's broader harmonic cosmology is designed to answer. The soul, as a microcosmic harmonic structure, must possess within itself a formal proportion to every level of being; this is why its definition requires not Aristotle's actuality-of-body formula, but something more like the Neoplatonic doctrine of the soul as a self-moving, self-reflexive totality.

The microcosmic doctrine itself deserves careful attention in the context of Zorzi's project. The notion that the human being mirrors the universe—that *homo est microcosmus*—is ancient, appearing in Presocratic philosophy, developed in Platonic and Stoic thought, crystallized in Neoplatonism and medieval Aristotelianism, and transmitted through Islamic philosophy into medieval European thought. By Zorzi's time, it was a commonplace of learned discourse.

Yet Zorzi's harmonic inflection of the microcosmic doctrine is distinctive. Rather than understanding the microcosm simply as a scaled-down replica of the macrocosm (the human body corresponding to the terrestrial sphere, the faculties to the celestial spheres, reason to the intelligible realm), Zorzi emphasizes the *harmonic proportionality* binding microcosm to macrocosm. The human soul, properly understood, is not merely a miniaturized copy of the cosmic order but rather a harmonic instrument capable of resonating with every level of the cosmic hierarchy. Just as a musical instrument with properly tuned strings can vibrate sympathetically with any tone that is sounded, the soul, properly proportioned, can attune itself to any intelligible reality.

This understanding explains the soul's capacity for universal understanding. The soul understands all things not by transcending its human nature and becoming something else, but by actualizing the harmonic potential that is inherent in its very nature. The soul *is* a chord, a complex harmonic unity, and this harmonic structure replicates in miniature the harmonic structure of the cosmos. Thus, in knowing itself, the soul simultaneously knows the cosmos. In understanding the harmonic relations that constitute its own nature, the soul understands the harmonic relations that constitute all being.

The mention of the soul's *symbolum* points forward to the sefirotic and harmonic vocabulary Zorzi will deploy throughout the *Tonus Secundus*. The term *symbolum* in Neoplatonic philosophy designates not merely a sign or arbitrary conventional mark, but a *formal resemblance* (*similitude*) that implies a real participatory connection. The soul bears a symbolic relation to intelligible realities in the sense that it participates in them; the symbol is the visible trace of the higher reality within the lower order. Just as the image of the sun in water participates in the actual sun without being the sun, the soul's symbols—its faculties, its virtues, its harmonic proportions—are participatory images of the intelligible realities and divine attributes they reflect.

The term also connects, as Zorzi would have understood it, to the Kabbalistic notion that each level of the cosmic hierarchy bears formal traces (*reshimot*) of the levels above it, enabling a *participatory epistemology* in which like knows like across the chain of being. In Kabbalah, the material universe is not a fallen realm separated from the divine but rather the final externalization of the divine emanations (*sefirot*). Each sefira contains an imprint (*reshimah*) of all the others, and the human soul, as a microcosm, contains within itself all the sefirot in miniature. Thus, by understanding the harmonic structure of one's own soul, one can ascend through contemplative practice to communion with the higher divine principles.

This is the metaphysical infrastructure Zorzi is laying in this opening chapter. By establishing the soul's essential unknowability and the necessity of indirect, analogical, harmonic approaches to its understanding, Zorzi has set the stage for the *Tonus Secundus* to proceed via harmonic analysis rather than via logical definition. The reader is now prepared to understand that the question *Quid sit anima*—What is the soul?—will be answered not by a formula or definition but by a progressive revelation of the soul's harmonic proportions, its symbolic resonances with all cosmic realities, and its participation in the divine order that structures all being.
